titlestmt.author: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives staff
titlestmt.sponsor: This project was made possible by a grant from the Council on Library and Information Resources (CLIR)

publicationstmt.publisher: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections & Archives

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-06-17 01:00:02 -0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

change.item: Encoded in EAD by Melissa McMullen

repository.corpname: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections & Archives

did.abstract: The Capital Jury Project (CJP) was started in 1991 under the direction of principal investigator, William J. Bowers. The project was funded by a NSF grant. There are three phases to the project.
did.physloc: The materials are located onsite in the department.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Processed in 2015 M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives staff.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, all of sub-series 1.1, 1.3, 3.1, and 3.3 are currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 1.1 and 1.3 will be fully open in 2079, and sub-series 3.1 and 3.3 in 2086. Archivists also separated and closed portions of a handful of files in subseries 1.2 and 3.2, and series 2 and 5 to fulfill the guidelines of the original NSF grant. The date the separated material will open to researchers is noted within each respective file.
All other material in the collection are open and unrestricted.

As an alternative to accessing the fully closed sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, which is not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

userestrict.head: Copyright
userestrict.p: This page may contain links to digital objects. Access to these images and the technical capacity to download them does not imply permission for re-use. Digital objects may be used freely for personal reference use, referred to, or linked to from other web sites.
Researchers do not have permission to publish or disseminate material from these collections without permission from an archivist and/or the copyright holder.

The researcher assumes full responsibility for conforming to the laws of copyright. Some materials in these collections may be protected by the U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) and/or by the copyright or neighboring-rights laws of other nations. More information about U.S. Copyright is provided by the Copyright Office. Additionally, re-use may be restricted by terms of University Libraries gift or purchase agreements, donor restrictions, privacy and publicity rights, licensing and trademarks.
The Department of Special Collections and Archives is eager to hear from any copyright owners who are not properly identified so that appropriate information may be provided in the future.

acqinfo.head: Acquisition Information
acqinfo.p: All items in this collection were transferred to the University Libraries, M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives in 2005, 2014, and 2015 by members of the Capital Jury Project at Norteastern and the University at Albany. In 2017, Deborah Good, former spouse of Bill Bowers, donated a small amount of additional correspondence, reports, data studies, and administrative files that have not been interfiled.

bioghist.head: Historical Note
bioghist.p: The Capital Jury Project was initiated in 1991 by a consortium of university-based researchers with support from the National Science Foundation. The Project was administered nationally by Dr. William Bowers, principal research scientist, Northeastern University.

Phase I of the Project covered 353 capital trials in 14 states. The Project continued with the Capital Jury Project II (CJP2). The second phase, CJP2 ran from 2001 to roughly 2005 when the third phase of the project started. The second phase was housed at the University at Albany's School of Criminal Justice's Capital Punishment Research Initiative, part of the Hindelang Criminal Justice Research Center. The main tenants of the research project were to (1) to build and maintain a national archive for historical documents and data on the death penalty; (2) to plan and conduct basic and policy related research on capital punishment; and (3) to encourage scholarship, conduct graduate and undergraduate training, and disseminate scientifically grounded knowledge about the ultimate penal sanction.

The principal investigator of the Capital Jury Project was William J. Bowers (1935-2017). Professor Bowers received his Ph.D. from Columbia University in 1966. He was principal research scientist in the School of Criminal Justice, University at Albany, and formerly of the faculty of Northeastern University. For his research on capital punishment, in 2000 Dr. Bowers received the American Society of Criminology's August Vollmer Award for outstanding contributions in applied criminology.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Capital Jury Project records document the phases of the Capital Jury Research project. The collection contains materials ranging from 1941 to 2011, the bulk of the material is from 1991 to 2011. The collection is divided into parts that represent the individual phases of the project. Also represented in the collection are publications and research output from the findings as well as research on capital punishment in the United States. Much of the collection focuses on the first phase of the Capital Jury Project.

arrangement.head: Arrangement of the Collection
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged into eight series.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Preferred citation for this material is as follows:

Identification of specific item, series, box, folder, Capital Jury Project, 1941-2011. M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives, University Libraries, University at Albany, State University of New York (hereafter referred to as the Capital Jury Project Records).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains publications produced from the data collected in the Capital Jury Project as well as other publications.

did.unittitle: "A Comparison of the Work of Thorsten Sellin and Isaac Ehrlich on the Different Effect of Capital Punishment" by David C. Baldus and James W.L. Cole
"Executions Seen Selling into Violence" by Jean Dietz
"Arbitrariness and Discrimination Under Post-Furman Capital Statues" by William J. Bowers and Glenn Pierce
"Deciding Who Dies" by Stephen Gillers and Other Death Penalty Research
"Executions in America" - Draft by Henry Schwarzschild
"Executions in the United States, 1608-1987: The Espy File" by M. Watt Espy and John Ortiz Smykla
Female Offenders on Death Row, Articles by Victor L. Streib
Capital Punishment of Female Offenders Articles by Victor L. Streib
"The Adventures of an Abolitionist: 13 Years Against Executions" by Neil D. Hulkower
"Californians Want an Alternate to the Death Penalty" By William J. Bowers LA Times Op Ed Article

Cross- Overs Capital Jurors Who First Believe the Defendant Deserves to Die (Live) and then Vote for Life (Death) by Marla Sandys
"Aggravating Circumstances and Capital Punishment Law: Rhetoric and Real Reforms" By James R. Acker and C.S. Lanier
"Capital Murder From Benefit of Clergy to Bifurcated Trials: Narrowing the Class of Offenses Punishable by Death" by James R. Acker and C.S. Lanier
"Capital Punishment and Contemporary Values: People's Misgivings and the Courts Misconceptions" by William Bowers
"The Dimensions of Capital Murder" by Jamess R/ acker and C.S. Lanier
"Doing the Devil's Work: Toward Model Death Penalty Legislation by James Acker and C.S. Lanier

"In Fairness and Mercy: Statutory Mitigating Factors in Capital Punishment Laws" by James R. Acker and Charles S. Lanier

"Family Histories of Death Row Inmates" From Grant Application and MR; mv-12/20/90; mv 11/20/91

"Judge: Death Penalty Kills Innocent" by Adam Gorlick
"Race and the Punishment of Criminal Violence" by Bowers, William J and Sandys, Marla

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subject files are some of the earilest material in the collection. The subject files came from the filing cabinets of Professor Bowers at Northeastern University. The material spans a great deal of interests and topics such ranging from Amnesty International to lethal injection to cameras in court.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Death Penalty News Clippings are clippings Professor Bowers collected for a number of years.
